speaker: interviewer
text: So we were up to you going back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We actually were back one night. We escaped through the borders. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We had a man who escorted us. Us, our family, and there were some other people also. We were a group, about 10. We started to walk in the night, and after about 10 minutes, we were attacked by the village robbers, I should call them. Because there were some people, you will be amazed, who made business over there and who had certain money with them. I don't know, but they wanted that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody ran away. They came with sticks. They, they yelled. We stood together, the family, and left my family's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: My father showed them his, his-- what the horse is wearing, such form of a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A horseshoe.

speaker: interviewee
text: A horseshoe. Well, it's nothing. It's a-- anyway, that he would give the money. Purse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a purse. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. And he showed them there's nothing in it. There was not one penny in it. We didn't have nothing, absolute nothing. Nothing, because we sold the last thing, because every, every little thing had a worth, had-- it was worth something. And we needed food. So we had just the clothes on us. Nothing-- Father had the tallit. The tallit [INAUDIBLE]. You know, the tallit, praying shawl. And he-- They wanted to take my mother's coat, because it was-- the night was cold. And somehow we managed to get friendly with them, and they left it. They didn't to anything to us. They didn't have anything. It wasn't a world like here, they should kill for nothing. They didn't have an [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stood there, the four of us. We didn't know what's happening. Finally, the man who escorted us came. And then we-- took us a couple of hours through mountains, through water, through everything. We get to a Hungarian village. What was the name of the village? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], Hungarian village, by the border. And she took us-- he took us, a Jewish family. But the Jewish family were told, if you take in somebody who comes over the border, you are going to be deported or whatever. So it was very dangerous, but they took us in, and they told us in the morning we have to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So early in the morning, I woke up. I left my father and mother in the-- where the cows are, in the stall. 'Stall' is it called? Anyway, and I went in the town to do something. We have to go home, right? And I went to the Russia court, at the-- leader of the Jewish people over there. And I told them, look, give me some money. My parents are there. We want to-- we want to take a train and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: He couldn't do anything. He's very exposed. This is very dangerous. He's a known man. Finally, he put me in his-- had a-- such a shack with-- where he, he, he kept the wood for the stove-- because my shoes were ruined. He sent the shoes to a shoemaker, left me there, not in his house. But anyway, I got a breakfast over there, which I never forget. Anyway. And he gave me as much money that I myself could go to the next town, which was Munkacs, which was Czechoslovakia. That time was Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's-- that's what happened. Meanwhile, my little brother run after me, said why did you come, why did left our parents over there. I can't do nothing to you. I don't have the money to take you. I can hardly get as much.

speaker: interviewee
text: She [INAUDIBLE] the packet that shows me I had money. I don't know how. It's very, very clever little boy. He's a very good businessman, and he can beat me. And it was Shabbat. The Jews advise us to go on Shabbat. Yeah, we took the train. We came to Satmar. I talked to the family. One man was with us, one man who had eight children. He was in Poland, and the whole family was in Munkacs, because he wasn't officially married with his wife. Now that, only [NON-ENGLISH]. So he was a single man. They didn't touch his family. I was by them. I spent the Shabbat by them. They were extremely nice. And I told the boy, you go down in there. They had a 20-year-old man over there, a boy. Your father is there, and your-- my parents are there. But meanwhile, the family didn't want to keep my parents, and they had to walk out. As they walk through the town, some people denounced them. So they were caught. They were put-- they were taken to the police over there. When I came home, I find over there by the police. I went back. [NON-ENGLISH]. Nobody would understand that. Anyway, I got back. They weren't there already. They were sent to Satmar because they belonged there. That was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were-- they were judged. There was a trail. They didn't know about me. And they had to stay [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four weeks in jail. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember seeing my mother in those-- those-- those garbs, you know? But they-- she-- but in the-- I never was in the-- you know, the stripes. And-- the picture is always in front of me, my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I took them kosher food every day in the jail. They didn't know that they are my parents. I told them just friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I came back, too, you see? But I think according to those rules there, I think I still was a minor. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, after four weeks, I thought they are going to let them out, so they send them to [INAUDIBLE], which is kind of a camp where they kept the foreigners. It wasn't a jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were there one year each, separate, separate. But they were all right. That was supported by the Joint. They had kosher food there and everything. It was-- it was not bad. They just weren't home, but they came to themselves, they gained weight, you see. And I was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] going from one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] relative to the other It was one of the most-- after the Holocaust and after Poland-- the worst period in my life, because I was [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what that means? Homeless. Homeless, absolutely. In the word's full meaning, homeless. And they were also afraid of me, you see? I was illegal somehow. And I was a little time here, a couple of weeks there, and a couple of months by another place. Just-- How did you support yourself during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that wasn't a problem. First of all, I was by the relatives, and then I had my sister in Hungary who sent me some money. I had my sister in Romania, in Bucharest, who was able to send some money. Not too much, but that was-- the money wasn't a problem. It was a problem where to stay, because I was-- I was absolutely alone, you see. My brother went to another brother. That was a time when you can't keep two-- two members of a family. He was young, married. He had small children. There wasn't money for it. There wasn't room for it. It, it, it was a completely, completely different world. And-- but I was for a while by an uncle, by another uncle, and by a cousin. All over. No good. Very bad, very bad. Very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then mama came home after a year. It was already '43, yeah? And we were home a year. We never build back our home. Never, never, never. All the furniture and everything was all over. We had a big house. We had a lot of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: We just rented a small apartment, one room, you know, a kitchen. And we went through-- mama knew how to sew. I also know how to sew. So we sewed a little. We made-- we made a living. It wasn't a problem. It was a problem that everything was already-- Then I went to visit my sister in Budapest. I stayed there for a while. And then we had the news that, that people are going in the ghetto. And I went home to be with my parents. My sister had her husband that time. I stayed with my parents just for a while, and then we were taken to the ghetto. Again, packing. I mean, that time, we didn't have-- that was not a real home, but we left and we went to the ghetto. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to bring anything with you to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: As much as we could carry.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you could carry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Some clothes, some food, maybe a pillow. Not, not, not too much. We didn't need too much. We didn't have room where to put it. We were 10 families in one, in one apartment. On the floor, all over, all over, very, very packed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was already such a terrible time. We didn't know what's going on. We didn't understand what's going on. What's happening here? What's this? You know? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto life was very terrible. We didn't have enough food, of course. I think-- I think they gave us the daily bread, somehow, the ration. But-- and the Jewish-- the-- there were also a kind of a Judenrat, which tried to provide whatever. But it was-- it was very, very, very hard. Very, very, very hard. And all kind of-- well, before. All kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: While we were in the ghetto, it was already nothing. But before the ghetto, you see, everything was restricted. We had-- we had to wear the yellow star on our arms and in the front. See? Should-- we should be able to be [INAUDIBLE] from far away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and then, of course, they took away the stores. Of course, we couldn't work. Of course, we couldn't leave our town. We couldn't go on the street after 7 o'clock. We couldn't go in the market ater-- I, I don't-- until noontime when there has nothing to buy anymore. Oh, thousands of restrictions. Restrictions, restrictions. It was unhuman, unhuman. The ghetto, we were all Jewish people there, and, and that didn't matter anymore, anything. There, there was no possibility for escape or anything like that. And we were terrible crowded, you see, and hungry. And then we didn't know where our families are, and we didn't have already the connection. I, I think we still got some of that. I remember my sister from Bucharest told me that he got a cart, which my father rode, actually. And he told him that this time I have a very bad week. My father had that, that instinct, that feeling. He felt things, if it's going to be good or bad somehow. Says when I went to Poland, I didn't have that bad feeling. No, it's something very worse. Something very bad is expecting us. So that, that was it. We stayed in ghetto not too long. I think about two months. Two months, terrible time, Very bad time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you told that you had to leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were always signs, always posters, you know, that we should stay, you know, doorways, whatever, with as much as we can carry, right? And we are going to be taken. So that, that, that was not too much time, but that didn't matter already. We didn't have-- in the ghetto, we didn't have to think what to take, what not to. We had so little anyway. We took whatever we took. Not, never pass-- but before we went to the ghetto, we were all searched, you see. The-- they searched the women, women. They put their-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what's the woman who helps-- who helps the mothers to get the children?

speaker: interviewer
text: A midwife.

speaker: interviewee
text: A midwife, yeah. A midwife search the woman. So what should I tell you? So we didn't have too much. But then, then we stood in our doorways, and then they-- we just-- we walked, you see. We walked in the ghetto, too. We walked. We looked terrible. That people, that bunch of people, you know, old people, children, infants, handicaps, all kind of-- you know, they, they took some-- they're blind people. Such a sight. It's, it's-- I can't described it. I remember it made a terrible impression when I saw that. With the-- with the-- you know, between people, there are all kind. With the luggages, with the packages, with, with just boxes, a couple of boxes, with just bundles on their back, because everybody tries to, to take whatever they can. A terrible, terrible, very sad sight is it. And then the gendarmes always, always behind us. Go on, go on, go on. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Those things are unbelievable. Now as I look back, it hurts even more. I, I think I, I couldn't [INAUDIBLE] at that time so much, but that sight was, was just really terrible. You know, some mothers has one child in the back, and one child in the arm, and one child-- very few mothers had carriages. It was expensive. Only the well-to-do could, could afford that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how we went to the ghetto, and that's how we left the ghetto. Went to the railroad station exactly the same way, exact-- through the city. And everybody was watching us.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say anything to you when you were walking to the railroad station?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were in the middle of the road. They were in their doorways, in their windows. No, no. Anyway, in Satmar we were a little bit strangers, because we lived there only several years, you see. So we really didn't have close Christian friends. We might have in our own town, but not here. But they tried to, to look from the windows and from the doors.

speaker: interviewer
text: They should hide away. They shouldn't stay there. That was, that was very disturbing. And I don't know. I think we felt ashamed or something, you see? However, it, it shouldn't be that way, but that's how-- we are, humans, you see? Something, such thing happened, maybe we think maybe, maybe we did something wrong, or who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of praying that time, but looks like sometimes God is also distracted. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the railroad station, they took you how?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the railroad station, we were-- the doors were open. They did the, the-- what do you call the-- those trains?

speaker: interviewer
text: The cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they called them the freight trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freight trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Freight trains. And we were pushed in, as many as they could. That was also-- because we, we, we didn't expect that yet. You see, if you-- if you don't live through such things, you can't imagine that such a thing could happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are dazed. You are confused. What's going on here? What's happening here? Oh, one after another. Fast, fast. One after another. But 18 freight. It's a known thing by now, you see? We couldn't stretch our limbs. We didn't have room. We didn't have air. [INAUDIBLE] One pail of water, and one-- the other pail for the toilet purposes. Man, woman, religious people, you know, children, all-- everything together over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyrhing, everybody express themselves the way they could, you know, by praying, by cursing, by yelling , by crying. All kind of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that freight, that three days till Auschwitz. Like just unhuman. Just you-- just-- even now, even I lived that through, it's, it's hard for me to, to-- how could such thing-- how could they make such thing, you know? So we, we put our sheet around us and we changed, or we did whatever we had to do. And we went one by one to the window to catch a little air. There's a small, little window with rails. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah. Sometimes we are very strong. Sometimes we just can't endure. We lived through that. Two people died there, and we had a baby born that time. Listen, how. How that is nature's way, right? That's how it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who they were who died. I didn't know them. But she took the baby with her, that woman. Of course, she didn't survive. With a baby, you never survived, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the arrival in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. That was such a terrible thing, you know. So noisy. Everybody was yelling, screaming. Dogs were barking. You know, men with, with, with striped garbs. The German soldiers were there, Nazi with dogs, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they opened the door. You know Down, fast, fast. Raus, raus. That was the German word for, for 'fast,' which is-- what is the exact translation? [NON-ENGLISH] I don't know. Move. Move, move it. Move, that would be the exact translation. So, so-- some Jewish-- they were Jewish man with their garbs, you know. They took us down. Leave everything behind. But that's all what we had. We was really reluctant to leave that behind. But nothing helped. Just what you have on you. Just-- So, we went all down. And then right away, men separate. Stay five in a line. So I was separated from my little brother and from my father. And that was the last time I saw my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went five in a row where they were instructed to go, not too long. And there there was the famous Mengele, Dr. Mengele, who made the selection. Now, I know. That time, I didn't know what's going on. Absolutely, I didn't have the slightest idea. Nobody had. We were all dazed, confused. We just, we just, we just didn't know what's going on. And he just divided us. The, the young separate. The old separate. Mothers with children went right away to the left side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- well, I wasn't a child then. Some girls cried because they were-- they were only 15 or 13. Sometimes even developed, they couldn't tell the age, and they, they cried after their mother. Somehow I didn't cry. I was always optimistic, and I was, I think, almost sure that we're taken care of. But the younger people are going to work, so what's the tragedy? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was the rest of it. I always, always, always remember my mother's last look. She looked after me. I locked back, she was standing there. And in her eyes, I saw the tragedy. I myself didn't understand. But in mother's eyes, so much sadness. So much worry that that scared me somehow, you see. Well, they all went. And then-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] We had to rush constantly, always. Raus, raus, raus. Fast, fast, fast. So we were-- got to a-- to a place [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where we had to undress. Undress completely. We had to stay naked. Naked, in row. You know, we had among us very, very religious, nice, young women or girls, you know. And they had to see the men were among us, going and coming. It was such degrading, sad. I don't know even I, I can find the right word to describe it. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As we stood in the row, one by one-- they had about 10 chairs. We sat on the chairs, and men came, and cut our hair from all our body, all over. All over, private parts, everything. Men. And when they cut my hair. You know, up till then, I just thought-- then I thought, well, finished. This is the end of the world. To cut my hair. I don't know why. I always liked my hair. They cut the hair with all bald. We looked at each other. I cried. That was the first-- Until then, I couldn't cry. I didn't know. I absolutely didn't know what's going on. But to cut the hair for a young girl. At that time, it was nice to have long hair. Well, that was until they cut our hair. And then another line, and they just throw to us the whatever, a dress or something. They just throw it to us, one dress and one panties, one pair of panties.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had to pass by. They throw it, and they mostly didn't fit, so after that, we changed it among us, the dresses. I was big, and they give me wasn't good, you-- somebody got a bigger one, who knows? But they gave us back. We went there with the nicest stuff what we had. Nobody took the schmattas, the really cheap things. But what we had there, it was only the cheapest stuff, and who care? We had the pass by our German [INAUDIBLE]. She was the-- what should I call her? Who over looked things over there. And we had to lift up our dresses to show her that we have only one panty, not two.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't understand what she tells me. She said to pick up my dress. I'm always distracted. It's my nature. I should see what's going on in front of me because they all did it. I-- so she hit me, and then somebody else told me, pick up your dress. Anyway, that was it. That was the introduction to Auschwitz. We didn't know where we are. We didn't know anything. And then were ushered to a barrack. Oh, there were two rows of barracks, about 10, 15 each side. Huge barracks. We were ushered in one of them. The barrack was completely empty with cement floor. We had to sit down and push our knees to our chest, so we should have enough room for everybody. That was the first. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How they-- anyway, I lost the word. We sit there, and, and-- exhausted, and tired, and upset, and whatever you want. We spent the night there. And we stayed in that barrack for a while. Two days-- two, three days later, I-- we ventured out. Of course, in the daytime. Sometimes it was so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. When the block was closed, we couldn't get out. And sometimes they let us out, just fresh air, and then we looked for our friends, for our relatives, for our neighbors, for-- you know. We were about a thousand in one block. Being there just a couple of days, I found a very dear friend of mine who actually lived in Slovakia, but she used to come every summer to a relative of hers. Spent the summer in our neighbor's. We were neighbors, and I was friends to her-- I was a friend to her cousin. She became very, very friendly. She was a lovely, lovely girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, oh, Gala, what's going on here? Where are we? What, what, what's-- where are my parents? Where are my nieces? She says, you mean you don't know? I said I just came a day before yesterday. I, I, I don't know. Well, she started to tell me that the, the parents are gassed, and after that, their bodies are cremated.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was working in an office over there. She was an office girl, kind of a secretary. She knew everything, of course. And she was telling me about-- she told me about atrocities, what's going on over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I looked at her. I, I couldn't grasp it. I couldn't understand. She says, Irene, don't you see? I said, that, that couldn't be true. I can't believe it. She says, don't you see the chimneys? They're smoking day and night, and those are our bodies from our loved ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know-- I know that she's telling the truth, but I couldn't-- I couldn't-- I just-- I just-- it didn't-- it didn't-- it didn't even touch the surface of my-- of my mind. Or-- such thing couldn't happen. I just-- I just couldn't believe it. It took me, I think, months until I was able to, to understand and to, to, to believe that whole tragic story. Months. Yeah. Gala, such a nice girl. She, she didn't survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, it started, you see. When I was there, I couldn't believe it, so what could we expect before we went there? You see? Iwas there, I saw it, and I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz a couple of days later, a couple of weeks later, a couple-- of sleeping on the cement floor, we got finally another barrack, which, which were these-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bunkers. Three, three floor, each bunker. Each place, we had 10 people in one bed, like. And it was three floors. 10, 10, 10, one above the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now, and we'll continue.
